full_response:
Let  

 G = (V ,E ,w) with w : E → ℝ  

be a connected, undirected, edge–weighted graph.  
A maximum-spanning tree (MaxST) is a spanning tree T whose total weight  
 w(T)=∑e∈T w(e)  
is as large as possible among all spanning trees of G.

We give two equivalent ways to obtain a MaxST and prove them correct.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Negate-weights reduction
────────────────────────────────────────
Idea Run any minimum-spanning-tree (MST) algorithm on the graph with negated
weights.

Algorithm  
1. Construct G′=(V ,E ,w′) where w′(e)= −w(e) for every e∈E.  
2. Run your favorite MST algorithm (Kruskal, Prim, Borůvka, …) on G′ to
   obtain an MST T′.  
3. Output the same edge set T′; it is a maximum-spanning tree of G.

Proof of correctness  
For any spanning tree T of G  
 w′(T)=∑e∈T (−w(e)) = −w(T).  
Hence T maximises w(T)  ⇔  T minimises w′(T).
Since an MST of G′ minimises w′, it simultaneously maximises w, so T′ is a
MaxST of G. ∎

Complexity Identical to the chosen MST routine  
 • Kruskal with Union–Find:  O(E log E) time, Θ(V) space  
 • Prim with binary heap:     O(E log V) time, Θ(V) space  
 • … etc.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Direct “maximum–Kruskal” algorithm
────────────────────────────────────────
We can avoid weight negation by flipping the comparison operators.

MAX-KRUSKAL(G)  
1. sort E into non-increasing order of weight  
2. make-set(v) for every v∈V                ▹ Union–Find  
3. T ← ∅  
4. for e=(u,v) in sorted order  
5.   if find(u) ≠ find(v) then  
6.    T ← T ∪ {e}; union(u,v)  
7.   if |T| = |V|−1 then break  
8. return T

Cut–property for maximum weight  
For any cut (S ,V\S) of G, the heaviest edge crossing that cut is contained
in some MaxST.

Proof (exchange argument)  
Let T* be any MaxST and let e be the heaviest edge across the cut.  
If e∈T*, we are done. Otherwise T*∪{e} contains exactly one cycle C.
The cycle includes another edge f that also crosses the same cut.
Because e is the heaviest on the cut, w(e) ≥ w(f).
Tree T*=T*∪{e}−{f} is a spanning tree and  
 w(T*) ≥ w(T) (with equality only if w(e)=w(f)),  
so T* is also a MaxST. Hence some MaxST contains e. ∎

Correctness of MAX-KRUSKAL  
Lines 4–7 select edges exactly in the order prescribed by the cut property,
preserving acyclicity (Union–Find test). Hence the resulting set T is a
maximum-spanning tree.

Complexity  
• Sorting: O(E log E)  
• Union–Find operations: O(E α(V)) ≈ O(E)  
Total time: O(E log E); space: Θ(V+E).

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Prim variant
────────────────────────────────────────
Replace the min-priority queue in Prim by a max-priority queue: at every
iteration add the heaviest edge joining the current tree to a vertex outside
the tree. Using a binary heap the running time is O(E log V), or O(E+V log V)
with a Fibonacci heap.

The same cut property (largest edge over the cut) proves correctness.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  Summary
────────────────────────────────────────
A maximum-spanning tree can be obtained by simply negating all edge weights
and running any minimum-spanning-tree algorithm, or by running Kruskal/Prim
modified to prefer larger weights first. Either way, the time and space
bounds are the same as for ordinary MST algorithms.

\boxed{\text{Negate edge weights and run any MST algorithm (or, equivalently, run Kruskal/Prim in descending-weight order) to obtain a maximum-spanning tree in }O(E\log E)\text{ or }O(E\log V)\text{ time.}}  

Confidence: 0.58
extracted_answer: \text{Negate edge weights and run any MST algorithm (or, equivalently, run Kruskal/Prim in descending-weight order) to obtain a maximum-spanning tree in }O(E\log E)\text{ or }O(E\log V)\text{ time.}
